ir_specs.statement: The cp command copies files or, optionally, directories, and the copy is completely independent of the original.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: cp copies files or directories, and the copy is completely independent of the original.

ir_specs.statement: By default, cp does not copy directories.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: By default, cp does not copy directories unless recursive options are used.

ir_specs.statement: The cp --parents option forms each destination path by appending the specified source name to the target directory.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: cp --parents appends the specified source name under the destination directory.

ir_specs.statement: When copying recursively, cp --copy-contents copies the contents of special files as if they were regular files.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: cp --copy-contents treats special files like regular files during recursive copy and can be risky.
ir_specs.notes: The manual warns that this can hang on FIFOs or fill a destination file system when used on special files such as /dev/zero.

ir_specs.statement: The cp -P option copies symbolic links as symbolic links rather than copying the files they point to.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: cp -P preserves symlinks instead of following them.

ir_specs.statement: To move a file, mv ordinarily simply renames it.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: mv ordinarily performs a rename when moving a file.

ir_specs.statement: If a rename cannot work because the destination file system differs, mv falls back on copying as if by cp -a and then removes the original.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: Across file systems, mv falls back to copy plus removal.

ir_specs.statement: If a file cannot be renamed because the destination file system differs, mv --no-copy fails instead of copying and then removing the file.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: mv --no-copy disables copy-and-remove fallback across file systems.

ir_specs.statement: The mv --exchange option exchanges source and destination instead of renaming source to destination, and both files must exist.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: mv --exchange swaps two existing paths.

ir_specs.statement: The mkdir command creates each directory name in the order given.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: mkdir processes directory names in the order given.

ir_specs.statement: The mkdir command reports an error if a name already exists, unless -p is given and the name is a directory.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: mkdir errors on an existing path unless -p is used and the path is already a directory.

ir_specs.statement: The mkdir --mode option sets the file permission bits of created directories to the specified mode.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: mkdir --mode controls permission bits for created directories.

ir_specs.statement: The mkdir --parents option makes missing parent directories and does not change the file permission bits of existing parent directories.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: mkdir --parents creates missing parents but leaves existing parent permission bits unchanged.

ir_specs.statement: The ln command makes links between files; by default it makes hard links, and with -s it makes symbolic links.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: ln makes hard links by default and symbolic links with -s.

ir_specs.statement: Hard links cannot cross file system boundaries.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: Hard links are restricted to the same file system.

ir_specs.statement: There are no restrictions against creating dangling symbolic links.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: Dangling symbolic links are allowed.

ir_specs.statement: The ln --relative option makes symbolic links relative to the link location and is only valid with --symbolic.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: ln --relative is specifically for symbolic links and uses a relative target path.

ir_specs.statement: In canonicalize mode, readlink outputs an absolute file name with no repeated separators and no '.', '..', or symbolic link components.
ir_specs.source_section: 12.6 readlink: Print value of a symlink or canonical file name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: readlink canonicalize mode resolves a path into a cleaned absolute name.

ir_specs.statement: With readlink --canonicalize, if any component of the file name except the last one is missing or unavailable, readlink produces no output and exits with a nonzero exit code.
ir_specs.source_section: 12.6 readlink: Print value of a symlink or canonical file name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: readlink --canonicalize requires all path components except possibly the last one.

ir_specs.statement: With readlink --canonicalize-existing, if any component is missing or unavailable, readlink produces no output and exits with a nonzero exit code.
ir_specs.source_section: 12.6 readlink: Print value of a symlink or canonical file name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: readlink --canonicalize-existing requires all components to exist.

ir_specs.statement: The readlink --no-newline option does not print the output delimiter when a single file is specified.
ir_specs.source_section: 12.6 readlink: Print value of a symlink or canonical file name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: readlink --no-newline suppresses the delimiter for a single input file.

ir_specs.statement: The realpath command outputs an absolute file name that contains neither unnecessary '/', '.', '..', nor symbolic link components.
ir_specs.source_section: 18.5 realpath: Print the resolved file name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: realpath prints a fully resolved absolute path without redundant path components.

ir_specs.statement: The realpath --canonicalize-existing option fails if the named file does not exist.
ir_specs.source_section: 18.5 realpath: Print the resolved file name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: realpath --canonicalize-existing requires the target to exist.

ir_specs.statement: The realpath --relative-to option prints the resolved file names relative to the specified directory.
ir_specs.source_section: 18.5 realpath: Print the resolved file name
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: realpath --relative-to can emit a path relative to a chosen directory.

ir_specs.statement: By default, rm does not remove directories.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: rm needs explicit directory-related options for directory removal.

ir_specs.statement: Any attempt to remove a file whose last file name component is '.' or '..' is rejected without any prompting.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: rm rejects attempts to remove dot and dot-dot path components.

ir_specs.statement: The rm --force option ignores nonexistent files and missing operands, and never prompts the user.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: rm --force suppresses prompts and ignores missing inputs.

ir_specs.statement: The rm --preserve-root option fails upon attempts to remove the root directory '/' recursively, and this is the default behavior.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: rm protects '/' from recursive removal by default.

ir_specs.statement: The rmdir command removes empty directories.
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: rmdir is specifically for empty directories.

ir_specs.statement: With no file name arguments, df reports the space available on all currently mounted file systems.
ir_specs.source_section: 14.1 df: Report file system space usage
ir_specs.source_quote_or_summary: Without file arguments, df reports on currently mounted file systems.

question_specs.question: By default, `cp` creates another path name for the same file instead of an independent copy.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. cp command copies files or, optionally, directories, and the copy is completely independent of the original.

question_specs.question: By default, `cp` does not copy directories.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. By default, cp does not copy directories.

question_specs.question: The `cp --parents` option can place the source path under the target directory instead of flattening the file name.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. cp --parents option forms each destination path by appending the specified source name to the target directory.

question_specs.question: The `cp --copy-contents` option skips the contents of special files during recursive copies.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. When copying recursively, cp --copy-contents copies the contents of special files as if they were regular files.

question_specs.question: With `cp -P`, symbolic links in the source are copied as symbolic links.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. cp -P option copies symbolic links as symbolic links rather than copying the files they point to.

question_specs.question: When possible, `mv` ordinarily moves a file by renaming it.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. To move a file, mv ordinarily simply renames it.

question_specs.question: If a destination is on a different file system, `mv` always fails immediately and never copies the file.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. If a rename cannot work because the destination file system differs, mv falls back on copying as if by cp -a and then removes the original.

question_specs.question: The `mv --no-copy` option prevents fallback copying across file systems.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. If a file cannot be renamed because the destination file system differs, mv --no-copy fails instead of copying and then removing the file.

question_specs.question: The `mv --exchange` option can swap two paths even if one of them does not exist yet.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. mv --exchange option exchanges source and destination instead of renaming source to destination, and both files must exist.

question_specs.question: The `mkdir` command creates directory names in a random order chosen by the implementation.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. mkdir command creates each directory name in the order given.

question_specs.question: Without `-p`, `mkdir` reports an error if the target name already exists.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. mkdir command reports an error if a name already exists, unless -p is given and the name is a directory.

question_specs.question: The `mkdir --mode` option changes only the permissions of already existing parent directories.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. mkdir --mode option sets the file permission bits of created directories to the specified mode.

question_specs.question: The `mkdir --parents` option changes the permission bits of existing parent directories to match the new directory.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. mkdir --parents option makes missing parent directories and does not change the file permission bits of existing parent directories.

question_specs.question: By default, `ln` creates symbolic links, and `-s` switches it to hard-link mode.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. ln command makes links between files; by default it makes hard links, and with -s it makes symbolic links.

question_specs.question: Hard links cannot cross file system boundaries.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. Hard links cannot cross file system boundaries.

question_specs.question: GNU `ln` forbids creating a dangling symbolic link.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. There are no restrictions against creating dangling symbolic links.

question_specs.question: The `ln --relative` option is only valid together with `--symbolic`.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. ln --relative option makes symbolic links relative to the link location and is only valid with --symbolic.

question_specs.question: In canonicalize mode, `readlink` can output a path that still contains `.` or `..` components.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. In canonicalize mode, readlink outputs an absolute file name with no repeated separators and no '.', '..', or symbolic link components.

question_specs.question: With `readlink --canonicalize`, a missing last path component is allowed, but a missing earlier component is not.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. With readlink --canonicalize, if any component of the file name except the last one is missing or unavailable, readlink produces no output and exits with a nonzero exit code.

question_specs.question: With `readlink --canonicalize-existing`, every path component must exist.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. With readlink --canonicalize-existing, if any component is missing or unavailable, readlink produces no output and exits with a nonzero exit code.

question_specs.question: The `readlink --no-newline` option removes the output delimiter for every file even when multiple files are specified.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. readlink --no-newline option does not print the output delimiter when a single file is specified.

question_specs.question: The `realpath` command can leave symbolic link components unresolved in its output.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. realpath command produces an absolute file name that contains neither unnecessary '/', '.', '..', nor symbolic link components.

question_specs.question: The `realpath --canonicalize-existing` option fails if the named file does not exist.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. realpath --canonicalize-existing option fails if the named file does not exist.

question_specs.question: The `realpath --relative-to` option prints resolved file names relative to a specified directory.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. realpath --relative-to option prints the resolved file names relative to the specified directory.

question_specs.question: By default, `rm` removes directories the same way it removes regular files.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. By default, rm does not remove directories.

question_specs.question: Attempts to remove `.` or `..` with `rm` are rejected without prompting.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. Any attempt to remove a file whose last file name component is '.' or '..' is rejected without any prompting.

question_specs.question: The `rm --force` option ignores nonexistent files and missing operands, and it never prompts the user.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. rm --force option ignores nonexistent files and missing operands, and never prompts the user.

question_specs.question: With recursive removal, protecting `/` requires an explicit `rm --preserve-root` because GNU `rm` does not do that by default.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. rm --preserve-root option fails upon attempts to remove the root directory '/' recursively, and this is the default behavior.

question_specs.question: The `rmdir` command removes empty directories.
question_specs.explanation: Correct. rmdir command strips empty directories.

question_specs.question: With no file name arguments, `df` reports only on the current working directory.
question_specs.explanation: Incorrect. With no file name arguments, df reports the space available on all currently mounted file systems.
